speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photo, Mr. Weinreich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. Now, as you can see, this is the wedding of my youngest uncle John to its beautiful wife. Now, this is my mother. This is my father. This is my grandfather, Simcha Schreiber. This is Abys. This is Mina. And this is [? Avram Harsh, ?] the one which died on my hands in Russia. And these are family of the [? Kalah ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your grandfather Schreiber.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandfather Simcha Schreiber had a terrible fate. He run away with this family, with his second wife. He run with his second wife. And he run away with three sons and with his daughter-in-laws and six children. He ran away to Tarnow. And when they came to Tarnow, they were hidden by a Polish fellow, by a Polish man in a bunker, what they call a bunker in those days. And-- and my grandfather had a lot of money with him, a lot. He had it in gold coins, in American gold coins. He was a very rich man. And, you know, as the time was passing, a-- a piece of bread was getting dearer and dearer and dearer, you know. It was ridiculous, you know, because they were completely innocent. They were hidden in underneath the house. And nobody knew where they were. And they, the fellow, used to bring him bread. And every time he used to bring bread, there was bigger extortion, more money, more money, money. Finally, in the beginning-- in the beginning of 1945, just before the war finished, his money ran out. And the same man went to the Gestapo and told that the Jews are hiding in-- in-- in such and such a place. And they got them out, the whole lot who were alive, the whole lot because we've got witnesses. They got them out into the backyard. And my grandfather asked them to wait just a minute because he knew they going to shoot him. To-- so he wants to put on the tallit and [? twill, ?] you know, before he gets shot. And when my daughter was painting, you know, she asked be once what sort of a painting I would like. So I told her, make me a painting after Chagall of the Jew with the tallit and [? twill. ?] And this is exactly what my grandfather, my father looked like. Right. And the whole lot was shot on that-- on that backyard. And after the war, my uncle John, which was in the Polish army went back with a Russian officer to try and catch the fellow. They were going to hang him in the middle of the street. But as soon as he found out that they're looking for him, he ran away. He hid. And they couldn't find him. They were there for three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This photograph was given to me by Uncle Lester, which was given-- which was sent to him from Poland. All right. And this is an original, which is priceless. Now, this is my grandfather Simcha. This is my father Chaim. This is my mother Fani. Here I am as a baby, a one-year-old. This is my brother. That is grandfather, grandmother, Simcha's mother. This is Auntie Mina. And this is [? Avrum ?] [? Harsh ?]. That is my auntie. And that's my uncle. And as you can see, I was a baby on my mother's lap. And this is Malka. And this is Lester before he went to Israel, to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this photo is from 1928, is it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about the people in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very interesting photo. This man is the brother of my grandfather. He-- he actually lived in Australia. He lived in America. Then he lived in Australia. He came home to visit his family. And he took this boy, Lester, which his name at that time was [? Liza ?], back to Australia. He brought him to Australia. He said Simcha you given me one of your sons to go to Australia. And he took him. That's my brother. That's [? Abish ?]. And these people I don't know. This is family. But I don't know. But this is Malka. This I know for sure. And that's Mina.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about the people in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very interesting photo. And as you know, my Uncle Lester came to Sydney, Australia. He married a Jewish girl from the [? Whitman ?] family, very well known here, the family. And they went. And they came back-- I think it was 1935-- to Australia. And part of their honeymoon, they went to Paris. They went to London. And they came to Chrzanow. And you can imagine what a shock that was for her when she saw Chrzanow coming from Sydney. And now, this his Uncle Lester. That's his-- her husband. That's Uncle John. This is my mother. This is [? Avrum Hersh ?] Blumenfrucht, my uncle, my auntie. This is Bernard. I love that boy. And that's my auntie. That's their baby. That's their girl. And this is a cousin, Jen, which this is Simcha Schreiber, the patriarch of the family. And this is Simcha-- and this young boy is [? Jeremy ?]. He actually lives here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. These are the famous letters that you were speaking of in the video. Can you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct. This is the last letter which my father wrote to me to-- to [INAUDIBLE] to Russia. As you can see, it's Chrzanow. The date is the third of the six, '41. And Chaim, he addresses him Chaim. And as he still lived at home in [? Esterplatz, ?] which Germany was-- [? Esterplatz ?] was [SPEAKING GERMAN] Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very famous letter. I knew every word by heart. And here he writes, as you can see with so much affection, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. My dearest, darling, you know, wife, [? Friedel, ?] you know? And half the letter is for my-- for my mother. And half of the letter is for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is a most touching letter that you can imagine. And here he writes to be-- to be respectful to my wife-- to my mother. And so it'll be always good to me to learn Hebrew with the rabbi. And actually, here he says, farewell. He says-- in this letter, he says, farewell, that shortly we'll be going away. They're going to take us away from the city. And we don't know our fate. And my heart is bleeding. And-- and I'm full of pain. And I am-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, I'm alone like a-- like a-- like a stone. And with the last drop of my blood, I'm writing this letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a letter which is unforgettable. And actually speaking, this letter give me a lot of heart, and a lot of strength despite the fact that it was so tragic. Right? So this is the famous letter. It's one of them. I've got others. OK. These are the famous postcards from your brother. Can you tell us about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my brother was taken early in the piece. The Germans walked in. And they took able-bodied young men to Germany to work on the different factories. And my brother, as you can see, wrote in German, which was not his mother tongue, because otherwise it wouldn't go through the censor. And now we can see quite clearly where he was. Is this from Moses Weinreich written to my father Chaim, to Chrzanow. My father was still in Chrzanow. And it's here, RAB Lager 3 [? Sackrowf, ?] by [? Ogaborn ?], something like that. You know, so he was there. And he writes in here. And these letters, these cards were written to my-- to my father. And my father send them to us in Russia so that we could read it. And they are most-- they-- they-- they really and truly are ripping your heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit of what's said?

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can see underlined here. And he writes to his-- dear father, you know, send me some [GERMAN]-- you know, tobacco. He actually-- he never smoked, but probably at that time he started smoking. And he said, send me some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm so tired, he said. And he said, the middle of the day. I can't even lift my head. Right? And I'm working on the-- on the rail. You know? And a lot of my-- here, he writes, a lot of my friends are going on holidays. In other words, what he meant by it, the ones which are really weak, you know, they're going to take them to Auschwitz. Right? And then finally, they took him to Auschwitz as well. And I forgotten to tell you that my father died in Auschwitz. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have got an eye witness that my brother actually died in Auschwitz. And how he died was very tragic. He just sat down, he leaned against the wall, and he died of malnutrition and also dysentery. And that's how my brother died in Auschwitz. And for years, and years, and years, I was always hoping that I'll-- that I will see them coming through the door. And I-- I-- one of the reasons why I went to Poland back is to go to Auschwitz and to say Kaddish so that their souls will rest in peace finally, because they've got no [INAUDIBLE]. They've got nothing. And I felt it. If I don't say-- if I don't say Kaddish in that place that actually they died, I will not be doing my-- my duty as a son or my duty as a brother. And I really adored them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this particular document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular document is a-- from the school when I was in-- in-- in [INAUDIBLE]. What do you call it-- end of the year report. Right? As you can see, I was an excellent pupil. In literature, I had four-- you know, it was every-- every three months or whatever they give assessment. I was four-- five-- five. Five was the highest. And so I was fives. I had-- German was five. And history, I was three, five, four. [INAUDIBLE], I didn't take. Geography, I was excellent, fives. Arithmetic, I wasn't so good. Geometry, I was not-- I was good. Physics, I was four, four, four which was very good. Biology, I was very good. And altogether, you know, my-- my marks were very high for a fellow who didn't speak one word of Russian when he came to the school, and to reach such, you know, high standard in education was quite a feat. Believe me. I beat the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very impressive. OK. Tell us about the lady in these photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lady in these photographs, a friend of mine, a man, very lovely fellow. You know, he went to the army. And very shortly afterwards, he came back. And he was-- unfortunately, he tripped on a mine. And he lost both legs. And he was in the-- in the hospital. And later on, he met this wonderful, beautiful girl. And he married her. I was the best man in the wedding. And he insisted that he goes under the chuppah with his legs, with his false legs, without-- without a stick, without any props. And I was holding him. And we went under the chuppah together. And I was very, very fond of him. And she was a beautiful girl. And they both-- and they got visas to go to Palestine after the war. And this is what a beautiful girl with a beautiful, you know, with a beautiful child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about the people in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very good photo of my mother and my auntie, Mina. I was very, very close to Mina. And I loved him very much. And this is my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were wonderful sisters. Right? And that's all I can tell you about this photo. That this is-- I'm very fond of this photo because it's so clear and so nicely done. All right? Thank you. This is an older photograph. Can you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the only photo, which I've got with my brother together with me as children in Chrzanow. And this photo we got from my uncle-- from my uncle in Israel. You know? My mother sent him the photo. And that must have been somewhere in 1930. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- when I came to Australia, I asked him, could you send me some photos from home? And he sent me this photo. Do you know who took the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a beautiful photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the two people in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this was my teacher, [INAUDIBLE]. He was teaching us Polish. Not only was he my teacher, but he was a distance family from Blumenfrucht-- from [INAUDIBLE] Blumenfrucht. Mr. Blumenfrucht, my uncle, and Mrs. [? Lillianthal-- ?] his name was [? Lillinathal-- ?] were brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they looked after me. They sort of adopted me like a-- as a-- I was their only child, you know, a child. And they really and truly looked after me. And from him, I learned a lot about the world, what's going on in the world. And Mrs.-- he died many years ago in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mrs. [? Lillianthal ?] is alive and well in Tel Aviv. She's over 90. And her brain is as sharp as it was 50 years ago. And she's extremely fond of my wife as well, which I'm very grateful to her. And she's a woman of tremendous intelligence. And I love her very much. And when we go to Tel Aviv, I make a point in-- in seeing here. And by-- I don't know for some-- for some strange reason, I'm not a crier. And I don't cry very much. But when I see her, I cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an important document. Can you tell us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This little document was given to us in [? Kyzylorda ?] to my mother and me. As you can see, the name is completely misspelled because that was a Russian, you know, fellow, which-- which wrote out those things. And he could hardly write, you know, Polish. And so the-- the name is completely misspelled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but from-- on this little piece of paper we traveled from [? Kyzylorda ?] to Poland. And this was our passport to-- to-- to freedom. And, you know, the funny part of the lot is this. The first word is in Polish. And the next one here is Russian. Right? But this little piece of paper gave us the passport to go. This is the passport to go to Poland. And in here you can see that I lived in [? Kyzylorda, ?] right? And [? Rayon, ?] that means like the-- like New South Wales. [INAUDIBLE] And here, actually I can tell you in which street I live in-- in [? Kyzylorda ?] [INAUDIBLE], number 20. This is a beautiful document. OK. Tell us about the person in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: The person in this photograph is my-- the only photo I've got of my father's brother. His name is [? Shloma ?], [? Shloma ?] Weinreich. He lived in Bielsko together with his wife and two girls. And he actually perished also in Auschwitz. And his wife and the two girls have-- I've got a photo of the two girls. We'll see later on-- have actually survived the war. And they went to Israel. And the two girls died very early in the peace when they came to Israel. From what I understand, one actually, you know, her mind went. And she committed suicide. And the other one died because, on both of these girls, from what I understood from my mother, the Germans made experiments. And they just did not survive very long after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does this photograph come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- from Bielsko, from a city called Bielsko.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the ladies that you were speaking of? Can you tell us the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. These are the names of my two beautiful cousins, Paula and-- I've forgotten the other one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Helen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen. Yes-- Paula and Helen. And they are my cousins from Bielsko. And these-- this is the only photo I've got of my cousins. And these are the girls which did not survive very long after the war, already with the best conditions, with the best help in Palestine, and later on, Israel, of course. And they just could not survive because their bodies would not-- did not take the punishment that they received during the war. And they died at a very early age, young girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is another member of the Schreiber family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my Uncle Bernard. I was tremendously fond of him. He was a joker. He was a happy, jolly man. And I really and truly loved this guy. And on top of it, I loved him because he had a colossal voice. He used to sing so beautifully. It was just a pleasure to listen to him. And he used to sing in the synagogue, in the-- in the little choir. And, you know, I used to love listening to him. And I was very proud of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about the people in this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very, very fond of this photo. And I'm very fond of the people in this photo. This is my mother's brother, Menachem. As you can see, he looks Palestinian-- you know, like a Jew from Israel. They lived in Haifa. Their grandfather sent them money to build a house, which I was telling you about. And as you can see, not only were my-- my family merchants and scholars, but they were also fighters. This fellow was in the Jewish brigade during the war. I met him-- I meet him every time I go to Israel. You know, I meet him. He's alive. He's well. And he told me once that he had four passports in his-- in his pocket and a million dollars. And he was-- he was one of the people which were responsible of taking the Jewish refugees to [INAUDIBLE] from German camps. And he was helping them on the way to Palestine by legal or illegal ways. And this is his mother, of course. This is Klara. She's still alive in-- in Israel. And unfortunately, his brother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the gentleman at the top?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Moishe. ?] [? Moishe ?] Schreiber, my cousin. And I'll tell you something else with him, that we are so much alike, that the first time when my wife saw him, she got a shock because we speak the same. We look the same. And when-- the last trip we went this year-- this year we were in Israel in the beginning of the year-- he took us for a drive, you know, in Haifa. And we were singing the same songs from home. And he never was [INAUDIBLE]. Because his father taught him. And my father taught me. And my grandfather taught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's quite remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's remarkable. My wife couldn't believe. She just could not believe it. He loves my wife too, loves her. You know, she-- we are very close with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this-- this photograph, this is my second cousin. His name is [? Summer ?] [? Goldberger ?]. And why I'm showing you this photo is for two reasons. First of all, I'm very proud to see that one-- that our family was fighters as well. I showed you [? Moishe ?] Schreiber. He was in the Israeli army. All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now this man was in the Polish army. And he was fighting. And was fighting right through the war up to-- in-- in Warsaw. In Warsaw he got shot. He lost one eye. And he-- he-- he was also here grazed with bullets. All right? And you see, when I look at him, I love it because we were fighting. Look at his gun on here on the side, right? And he send us-- actually, the process, he stole goods in-- in Warsaw. And he send us parcels to Russia. He was very fond of my mother. And Yom Kippur-- when Yom Kippur was here-- actually, after the war, he was given a colossal, big flat in the business in the mountains on-- in-- in Germany, you know, which the Poles took over, as I told you, Silesia. And the minute I came, he took me to the mountains where he had this business. And I-- I was there with him for one month to recuperate. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I will always remember him. He died. And unfortunately, he was here. He struggled at little bit as far as money was concerned and so forth. And then he-- he was a Zionist.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went back to-- to Israel. And from Israel, he went back to Poland, because his wife was Polish. And he had to look-- in Poland. And on his way back to Australia, the strain was so big for him that he had a heart attack in-- in Switzerland or in Holland. I assume in Holland. In the hotel, he had a heart attack and died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we brought his body in here to bury. His-- his heart could not take the stress, what he saw. And he was over the moon in Israel. And he was very stressed in Poland. I'm very fond of him. I was very fond of him.

words.text: I was very fond of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this document, Mr. Weinreich.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a registration card with the Jewish community in Krakow. When we arrive, as I told you, we had no money. We had no clothing. We had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- the-- the Americans used to send a little bit of money, a little bit of clothing to Poland. And it was in-- in a warehouse there with the Jewish committee. And there's two entries in here. One entry is help-- money help, which they give us 700 zlotys, which was nothing, very little, and a little bit of clothing, which I can't even tell you what they gave me, a little bit of-- probably a couple of shirts or something like that. So this is this. Now-- What have you here, Mr. Weinreich?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what the Polish people call [POLAND]. It was given out in Krakow [INAUDIBLE] in the 10th of the eight, '46. And misspelled the name again. And this is to certify that Joseph Weinreich, which was born in Chrzanow, the son of Chaim and Fani Weinreich-- and Fani [INAUDIBLE], and Fani Schreiber from home, who lives on such and such a street, has called into this office. And he's going to be inducted into the-- into the Polish army. And, of course, they will let you know when to come and to face, you know, whatever it is. And so this is a document to state that I was going to go to the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: This photograph is from an important day. Can you tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is one of my most happiest days of my life. As you can see that this was the wedding when I got married to Aneta, and my mother very proudly in the middle. And incredible how beautiful she was on that day. And she's beautiful till today, even more so. She's getting more beautiful every day. And I think we are a very lucky couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell us about this lovely picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a beautiful photo. I'm very fond of it. This is-- this, you can, see that's Lilian, Michelle, and Henry, children growing up when they're small kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is Lilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilian is the eldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Michelle is the middle one. And Henry's the youngest. She always asked me that, you know, she was sure whether she was adopted or not. [LAUGHS] It was really something. Because the middle child had always got some sort of-- you know, she wasn't sure. I said, I-- can I show you you are ours and we love you more than anything?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This would have been a very proud moment for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I told you, I had a few happy-- happy-- happy days of my life. And this was definitely one of them. I was so proud and so happy to see my beautiful, eldest daughter graduating from one of the best universities in the world, from Sydney University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And look how beautiful she looks in this-- in this outfit. You know? It's just incredible that from a-- I used to tell them, from a little fellow from Chrzanow to reach this height, it's plenty to be thankful for. Another happy moment for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Michelle was, at the time, in Los Angeles. And she came especially from Los Angeles for her graduation. And she said to me that she felt always that she's done the right thing. She wasn't sure if she wanted to come back or not for the graduation, you know, because it was a long trip. But let me tell you something. As you can see, I'm so proud that Michelle also graduated in political science. And she-- and both girls, intelligent, clever, wise, and warm-hearted. There's-- [INAUDIBLE] the best in the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the last member of the Weinreich family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our son, dearest and beloved son Henry at his graduation. He's handsome. He's clever. He's intelligent. He's got a heart of gold. He's full of goodness. And not only to us and not only to his sisters, but he's very humane. And there's nobody like him, anywhere in the world. I have traveled the world over. I've seen people. And, of course, I'm biased. But I can honestly say that there's nobody like him. And there's nobody like Michelle. And there's nobody like Lilian. There's just-- there's nothing-- people like this does not exist. And we have survived. Sometimes I feel that we have survived for one reason and one reason only, to have such lovely children.
